speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape six. It's May 31st, 1995. This is an interview with Olivia Beton Jackson. We're talking about your return to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, military trucks drove us from Germany to a town in Czechoslovakia. The name is Pilsner By the way, the famous beer, Pilsner Beer is made there. And from there, we made our-- we were just dumped by them. And then we had to make our own way home on all kinds of freight trains, we climbed onto. You have to realize that this is the end of the war. Trains are not running regularly. Freight trains on and off. It took us a few days to get to a city called Bratislava. Today's it's the capital of Slovakia. And I come from a small town, [NON-ENGLISH], which is right next to Bratislava. So we made our way to Bratislava, and then there we spent a week. And we met a hometown guy with a carriage with horses. You know, horse and buggy. And with that, we went back home, the three of us. When we got home, we found there were some people already back. All together, eventually, 35 people returned. But by now, there were 20-odd people home. And my father wasn't yet. And we came to our home. Our home was stripped. And even the doors were removed. Everything, it was stripped. So we borrowed some straw from a neighbor, from a farmer next door who, by the way, were very, very happy to see us, and very shocked to see us. They immediately gave us some milk to drink.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- we lived in the home for several weeks. There was a public kitchen set up, where we went to eat. And bit by bit, people were coming home. But my father hadn't come.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we found out that one of the young men from the town who was with him was coming home. His name was Lunger. Miche, or Mickey. By the way, this is the husband of that young woman who forgot her diapers in the train. Just by coincidence.

speaker: interviewee
text: We found out that he had been with my father in the same camp, and that he's coming home. And he'll be able to tell us where my father is. He arrived, and he said, oh, he's on his way. But Weiss, from [NON-ENGLISH]-- You remember that town where we were in the ghetto? He knows more where he is. So my brother was going to go to [NON-ENGLISH] to find out from Mr. Weiss where my father was. But that morning, instead of leaving, somebody was going with a horse and buggy to [NON-ENGLISH]. Early in the morning, he was leaving so my brother went to this man to go with him to [NON-ENGLISH], And then he came back. His face was ashen. And my mother said, so? He didn't go to [NON-ENGLISH]? So my brother says, he went. I didn't go. So why not? So my brother says, this man who was going to [NON-ENGLISH] said to me, look, sonny. Nobody wants to tell you, but your father is not coming home. They're sending you to [NON-ENGLISH] so they shouldn't have to tell you. So I'm telling you now. You shouldn't have to schlep all the way there. So my brother came home and told us. We knew that my father is not coming home. We knew that my Aunt Serena is not coming home. And then we decided to-- by the way, we sat down to sit Shiva for one hour. The law is that you sit Shiva only for one hour if death-- the notice of the death reaches you after a year. More than a year had elapsed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we found out that my father had died during the holiday of Passover. We don't know exactly how. Either he was shot, because Bergen-Belsen, where he had been, was evacuated. And he was in a very bad shape. And he couldn't line up anymore. And all those people who took long to go to the lineup were shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Mickey Lunger, or Miche, as a Hungarian would call, he ran back to the barracks to look for him, and found him in a pile of corpses. So we know the date when he died, and it is during the Passover holiday. And we also remembered that that same day, my mother and I had a reason, in Augsberg, [SOBS], to-- we cried all night, my mother and I, that day because something had happened to us which right now, I cannot tell you in order not to prolong the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: But by coincidence, that was the night that my father died. And then my mother, my brother and I, we got on a train, and we went to Hungary to look for relatives. We didn't find anyone. No one returned. So we came back to Czechoslovakia. And shortly afterwards, we decided to leave. We didn't want to stay any more in Czechoslovakia. But you see, in the meantime, my brother got papers as a student to go to America. We couldn't go to America, by the way, because our quota was still not in place. We went to Prague. We had to wait two more years on our quota to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we decided to go to Germany to a DP camp, because we heard that there are refugee boats leaving for the United States. I personally wanted to go to Palestine. I didn't want to go to any other country. I wanted to be in a Jewish state.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my brother had gone to America, and my mother didn't want to be separated again. So we said, three of us survived. The three of us, we want to remain together. And so we headed for Germany, to a DP camp, from where then, as refugees, we would be able to reach America. However, in 1948, in the spring, again in March, there was a military takeover. The communists took over the government and closed the borders of Czechoslovakia's. The American embassy packed up and left. And in order to get out, in 1949, my mother and I smuggled across the border. And that's a long story in itself. It was-- We escaped to Vienna, to the American zone with a transport of these underground-- we went through snowy mountains and forests, et cetera. It's a story in itself. At that time, I was already 18, and my mother was in her early 50s. We got to Vienna and the American zone. We stayed there for about eight months. And from there, we made our way to Germany, to a DP camp where, by the way, I enrolled in a school. The DP camp was Feldafing, not far from the place where we were liberated. And by the way, now when I was in Seeshaupt in Germany, I flew to Munich and I took a train to Seeshaupt. And as I was riding to Seeshaupt, I passed Feldafing. The train station. I couldn't believe it. I never knew, at the time, the area, exactly where it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were in this DP camp for two years before, on a refugee boat, we arrived in the United States in 1951. April 7, 1951, I arrived in the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: I completed elementary school in Germany on a private basis. And then-- and I have a document to show you with my picture on it. And then, in the United States, I passed the high school equivalency test and entered college as soon as we arrived. I went to Brooklyn College. I got my BA there. Continuing my studies was the most important thing to me. And I worked during the day, and I went to school at night. I got my BA from Brooklyn. My master's from New York University, and also my Ph.D. From New York University. My BA in Hebrew language. And my MA was in history, and so is my Ph.D. I have been teaching in CUNY, in the City University of New York for 30 years. I am now, perhaps, taking the incentive and retiring after 30 years, during which I have been a full professor at Lehman College for 27 years. I got married in the United States. Have two children. Avignon, Mark Avignon. Mark, named after my father. He's the eldest. He has four daughters. He's an ophthalmologist. His wife is an endocrinologist. My daughter, Emily, has three children. Two boys and a girl. I remarried after 15 years of my first marriage. My husband's name is Dr. Leonard Jackson. He's a physician, as well. I have two step-children who live in Israel. The older one, a son, is a neurosurgeon. And the younger one is a teacher in Israel. Now I divide my time between the United States of America and Israel. I have a dual citizenship finally. [SOBS] Finally, I live in a Jewish state part of the year, when I'm not teaching here. That was my dream all along. And 18 years ago, my husband, Len Jackson and myself made our home in Israel. And I commute between [NON-ENGLISH] and the Bronx several times a year, in order to live there and in order to continue my career as a professor of history here. I want to say that I am grateful to the United States for having given haven to us. And I only pray that the United States will recognize that Israel is very important to the Jewish people so that another Holocaust should never happen again. Because now there is a Jewish state where Jews can go when there is danger elsewhere. And also, there is a Jewish state who sticks up for Jews in those countries where they have no rules.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I am only pleading that the great country of the United States, no matter who the government is, will always see to it that Israel's survivor existence will not be jeopardized, with no political arrangement that could prove dangerous or hazardous to its health. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for doing this. OK. This is my family. I want to introduce them one-by-one. On the right is Adina. Adina is the third daughter of my son, Avi Avignon. Dr. Avignon Beton. And he's sitting next to Adina. This is Avi, he's my oldest. He's an ophthalmologist and lives in Queens. Next to him is Mikhail, the second of Avi's daughters. Mikhail is in 6th grade. 7Th grade, forgive me. This is Jael. This is my oldest granddaughter. She's in first year high school. She's also Avi's daughter. Next to me, to my left, is my husband Len Jackson. Dr. Leonard Jackson. Next to Len is Emily, my daughter. Emily Beton [NON-ENGLISH]. She's holding her youngest, Laura, on her lap. Next to Laura is sitting Yani. Yani is Emily's son, the oldest. Next to Yani is Yani's father, Emily's husband, Steve [NON-ENGLISH]. And Steve is holding Ariel, on his lap. So Ariel, Yani, and Laura are Emily's children. Avi's wife, Roshel, is not here. She's an endocrinologist, and she's working in a hospital. She's very busy, couldn't come. And they have a fourth daughter. An adorable Daniella. And she could not come, either. She had other engagements. She is now 22 months old. Only 21. 21 months old. So she's not here. And we miss her. Thank you. These are three editions of my book, entitled, 'Elli, Coming of Age in the Holocaust,' that I had written. In 1980, the first edition came out. And then the second one in paperback, in 1983. And the same year, the Dutch edition of my story during the Holocaust. In this picture, my mother is holding me. I'm one-year-old. And my brother is five, a little over five, on his tricycle in front of our store, in the small town, [NON-ENGLISH], where I come from.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one-year-old, it must have been in 1932. I was born in 1931.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you manage to keep it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. That's a good question. This particular picture was given to us by one of the neighbors. One of our Gentile neighbors who had it. There are other pictures, which we got different ways. And I'm going to explain. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a school picture taken in the school yard in [NON-ENGLISH]. I am seven years old in this, in 1938. I could be found right above the teacher, to the right of the teacher's head. [INAUDIBLE] teacher, by the way. I remember his name was Joseph. Joseph. This is my father. The picture was taken in the ghetto shortly before he was taken away. And he's very sad at this point. You can see in his face that he was really very, sad. Everyone had a picture taken, it seems, for ID purposes. Which eventually we never used. This is myself, also in the ghetto. I'm 13 years old here and I still have my braids. These pictures, actually I found after the war. I went to the photographer who I knew took those shots. And he had run away from the Russians. I had gone to his studio and looked through the negatives. And in those days, the negatives were put on glass. Blackened glass. And I found these two glass plates amongst thousands of thousands of plates. And that's how we developed them. And this is how I have these two shots, the one of my father and of myself. I didn't find any other pictures of any of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This picture. Should I speak already? This picture Here was taken in 1946, a year after we came home. We took it as a passport photo. We were hoping to get to America. In the middle is my mother. As I explained, she has her hair completely covered. And on her right is myself. And wearing a coat with a hood on, and on the left is my brother, finally wearing a gray velour hat.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean you're on the left, don't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it depends how you look at it. Yes. Look at it this way, it's yes. On the left. I said that, in the other picture, also I made a mistake then, with the teacher. I was really to the teacher's left, not to the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. Were you finished telling us about this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm to the left of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken shortly before my mother's death in Israel. It was my brother's daughter's wedding. And it was just about two months before she died at the age of 93 and a half. In Israel. This was in 1987.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, thank you for doing this. It was such an honor. [INAUDIBLE] Watch your step.

speaker: interviewer
text: Should I turn the mixer off? Should I turn the mixer off?

words.text: Should I turn the mixer off?
